---
#title: "DIA Methods"
#pagetitle: "PI_DATE"
author: Center For Proteomics and Metabolomics
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, echo = F, include = F}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(warning = F, message = F, fig.align = "center", echo = F, cache = F)
```


```{r logo, fig.align="center", out.width="40%"}
knitr::include_graphics(path = "/research/rgs01/applications/hpcf/authorized_apps/proteomics_apps/proteoDA2/CPM_Hz.png", rel_path = F)
```

[add variables for text]::

```{r}
instrument = instrument
SearchEngine = search_engine
database = database
db_version = db_version
filt_min_reps = filt_min_reps
filt_min_groups = filt_min_groups

```

##  **DIA Methods** 

### **Data Analysis** 

The raw mass spectrometry data from the `r instrument` were searched with `r SearchEngine` [1,2] using the library-free mode. Protein sequences were downloaded from the `r paste(database, organism)` database (`r db_version`). The protein FASTA file was used to build an in silico library with the following settings: digestion enzyme Trypsin/P with maximum 2 missed cleavage; Oxidation on Methionine as variable modification;carbamidomethylated (+57) as fixed modification; maximum number of variable modifications set to 2; peptide length from 7 to 30; precursor charge 1–4; precursor m/z from 400 to 1200; fragment m/z from 200 to 1800. 

The search parameters of `r SearchEngine` were set as follows: precursor FDR 1%; mass accuracy at MS1 and MS2 both set to 10 ppm; scan window set to 0 (automatic inference); isotopologues and MBR (Match-Between-Run) turned on; No shared spectra enabled; protein inference at gene level; heuristic protein inference enabled; quantification strategy set to Robust LC (high precision); neural network classifier single-pass mode; cross-run normalization turned off; Library generation set to smart profiling; Speed and RAM usage set to optimal results. 

The search results were further filtered with precursor q value <0.01 and protein group q value <0.01 at the library level. For quantification, DIA-NN first obtained precursor quantities by summing the intensities of the top six fragments (ranked by their library intensities) for each precursor. Precursors corresponding to unique proteins were then used for protein-level quantification and intensities of protein groups were obtained using the MaxLFQ algorithm implemented in the iq r package [3]. Missing values were filtered so that proteins required `r filt_min_reps` replicates to have values in `r filt_min_groups` sample groups. Statistical analysis was performed using proteoDA which performs linear models for microarray data (limma) with empirical Bayes (eBayes) smoothing to the standard errors [4,5]. P-values were adjusted using the false discovery rate by the Benjamini-Hochberg procedure. Proteins with an FDR adjusted p-value < 0.05 and a fold change > 2 were considered significant. 

## **Acknowledgements**

#### Center of Proteomics and Metabolomics at St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital

## **References** 

1. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31768060/
2. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35803928/
3. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31909781/
4. https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24753412/
5. proteoDA DOI: 10.21105/joss.05184
